exercises.title: How many instances receive the Pub/Sub message?
exercises.scenario: Pub/Sub fans out to all current subscribers.
exercises.instructions:
```
5 app instances, all subscribed to 'cache.invalidate.products'

Instance 1 executes:
  redis.convertAndSend('cache.invalidate.products', 'P001')
```

1. How many instances receive the 'P001' message?
2. Does Instance 1 receive its own message?
3. What if Instance 3 is restarting when the message is published?
4. How is this different from Kafka?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. ALL 5 instances receive the message (including Instance 1).
   PUBLISH fans out to ALL current subscribers simultaneously.

2. YES, Instance 1 also receives it (it's subscribed too).
   Typically: evict local BEFORE publishing to avoid double-eviction.
   Or: let the handler evict (it's idempotent — evicting twice is harmless).

3. Instance 3 (restarting) MISSES the message.
   Redis Pub/Sub is at-most-once: no persistence, no retry.
   Instance 3 restarts with empty L1 cache (clean start) — no problem.
   If Instance 3 was running but briefly disconnected: it misses the event,
   L1 remains stale until TTL expires.

4. Kafka difference:
   Kafka: durable log, messages persisted, consumer groups, replay.
   Pub/Sub: fire-and-forget, no persistence, fan-out to all subscribers.
   Kafka: Instance 3 reconnects and reads missed messages.
   Pub/Sub: Instance 3 permanently missed the message.

test_cases.expected:
1. ALL 5 instances receive the message (including Instance 1).
   PUBLISH fans out to ALL current subscribers simultaneously.

2. YES, Instance 1 also receives it (it's subscribed too).
   Typically: evict local BEFORE publishing to avoid double-eviction.
   Or: let the handler evict (it's idempotent — evicting twice is harmless).

3. Instance 3 (restarting) MISSES the message.
   Redis Pub/Sub is at-most-once: no persistence, no retry.
   Instance 3 restarts with empty L1 cache (clean start) — no problem.
   If Instance 3 was running but briefly disconnected: it misses the event,
   L1 remains stale until TTL expires.

4. Kafka difference:
   Kafka: durable log, messages persisted, consumer groups, replay.
   Pub/Sub: fire-and-forget, no persistence, fan-out to all subscribers.
   Kafka: Instance 3 reconnects and reads missed messages.
   Pub/Sub: Instance 3 permanently missed the message.

hints: Fan-out: every current subscriber gets the message
At-most-once: no persistence — offline subscribers miss messages
Pub/Sub vs Kafka: Pub/Sub = broadcast; Kafka = durable log

exercises.title: Complete the Pub/Sub configuration beans
exercises.scenario: Three beans needed: handler, adapter, container.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks for the Spring Pub/Sub configuration.

blanks.hint: Spring class that manages Redis subscriptions
Wildcard topic for pattern matching (vs ChannelTopic for exact)
Method name on CacheInvalidationHandler to call

hints: RedisMessageListenerContainer: the subscriber infrastructure
PatternTopic('*'): subscribes to all matching channels
MessageListenerAdapter second arg: method name to call on handler

exercises.title: Minimal Pub/Sub publisher and subscriber
exercises.scenario: Simplest possible Spring Data Redis Pub/Sub implementation.
exercises.instructions:
Write:
1. CacheInvalidationPublisher @Service: publish(channel, key) via convertAndSend
2. CacheInvalidationHandler @Component: onMessage(message) logs received key
3. PubSubConfig @Configuration: container + adapter beans
4. Show: how the three classes connect
hints: convertAndSend: Spring's PUBLISH wrapper
MessageListenerAdapter: bytes → String → method call bridge
Container wires the connection factory and channel subscription

exercises.title: Cache invalidation handler with CacheManager
exercises.scenario: Handler evicts from L1 Caffeine cache when Pub/Sub message arrives.
exercises.instructions:
Write CacheEvictionHandler that:
1. @Autowired CacheManager
2. onEvict(message, channel): extracts cache name from channel suffix
3. Evicts the specific key from that cache
4. If message is '*': clear all entries from the cache
5. Logs every eviction
hints: channel.lastIndexOf('.')+1: extracts suffix after last dot
cache.evict(key): removes specific entry; cache.clear(): removes all
null check: channel may not correspond to any configured cache

exercises.title: Publisher that evicts local + broadcasts
exercises.scenario: Complete invalidation: evict local L1 AND publish to all other instances.
exercises.instructions:
Write CacheInvalidationService:
1. invalidate(cacheName, key): evict from local + publish to channel
2. invalidateAll(cacheName): local clear + publish '*'
3. Channel format: 'cache.invalidate.{cacheName}'
4. Show: evict local FIRST, then publish (avoids serving stale between publish and evict)
hints: Local first: this instance is immediately consistent after evict()
convertAndSend fires after local evict: consistent ordering
'*' as broadcast message: handler clears entire cache on receiving '*'

exercises.title: Full Pub/Sub configuration with multiple caches
exercises.scenario: Real-world config subscribing to multiple cache invalidation channels.
exercises.instructions:
Write PubSubConfig for Flipkart with 3 cache channels:
1. 'cache.invalidate.products' → CacheEvictionHandler.onEvict
2. 'cache.invalidate.categories' → same handler
3. 'cache.invalidate.sellers' → same handler
4. Use PatternTopic to subscribe to all three with one subscription
5. Handler routes based on channel suffix
hints: PatternTopic covers multiple channels with one subscription
Handler with (message, channel) signature: Spring passes both when using PatternTopic
channel suffix routing: extract cache name from channel for correct eviction

exercises.title: Test Pub/Sub with CountDownLatch
exercises.scenario: Async Pub/Sub delivery requires synchronization in tests.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest test:
1. Put 'P001' in 'products' cache
2. Subscribe to messages with a test listener + CountDownLatch
3. Publish invalidation for P001
4. latch.await(2, SECONDS) — wait for subscriber
5. Assert: P001 is no longer in products cache
hints: Local evict is synchronous: asserting after local evict is reliable
Thread.sleep(50): small wait for async pub/sub delivery to other instances
Both tests verify that the CacheInvalidationService does what it should

exercises.title: ProductService with integrated cache invalidation
exercises.scenario: Service that combines Spring @Cache annotations with Pub/Sub broadcast.
exercises.instructions:
Write FlipkartProductService:
1. findById(id): @Cacheable('products', key='#id')
2. updateProduct(product): saves to DB, evicts local + publishes via CacheInvalidationService
3. deleteProduct(id): deletes from DB, evicts local + publishes '*' for categories too
4. createProduct(product): saves, publishes categories invalidation
5. Show: how @Cacheable and manual invalidation coexist
hints: @Cacheable for reads + manual invalidation for writes: clean separation
Categories need invalidateAll on create/delete: entire list is affected
Product invalidation: only specific ID evicted (cheaper than clear all)

exercises.title: Restaurant status broadcast — Swiggy real-time
exercises.scenario: Restaurant open/close status must propagate instantly to all instances.
exercises.instructions:
Write RestaurantStatusBroadcaster:
1. setOpen(restaurantId): update DB + publish 'restaurant.status.{id}' = 'OPEN'
2. setClosed(restaurantId): update DB + publish 'restaurant.status.{id}' = 'CLOSED'
3. Handler: on message, evict from 'restaurantStatus' L1 cache
4. Config: subscribe to 'restaurant.status.*' pattern
5. Explain: why is 30-second TTL NOT acceptable for restaurant status?
hints: Channel includes restaurantId: 'restaurant.status.R001' → handler extracts 'R001'
PatternTopic('restaurant.status.*'): handles all restaurant IDs
setClosed urgency: orders must stop immediately — TTL is too slow

exercises.title: What happens when subscriber misses the message?
exercises.scenario: At-most-once delivery: offline subscribers miss messages.
exercises.instructions:
```
Setup:
  5 instances, all subscribed to 'cache.invalidate.products'
  Each has product P001 in L1 Caffeine cache (TTL: 60s, 40s remaining)
  Instance 3 loses Redis connection for 200ms due to network blip

At T=0: Product P001 price updated on Instance 1
  → Instance 1: DB update, local L1 evict, PUBLISH
  → Instance 3: misses the message (disconnected)

At T=5s: Instance 3 reconnects

1. Which instances serve correct price (₹799) immediately after T=0?
2. What does Instance 3 serve between T=0 and T=40s?
3. What does Instance 3 serve after T=40s?
4. What is the maximum staleness window?
5. How could you reduce this window?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Instances 1, 2, 4, 5 → immediately serve ₹799 (L1 evicted + fresh load)
   Instance 3 → still serves ₹999 (stale L1, missed invalidation)

2. Instance 3 between T=0 and T=40s:
   Serves STALE ₹999 from L1 Caffeine cache.
   The cache TTL countdown continues — it was at 40s when the miss happened.
   Instance 3 reconnected at T=5s but Pub/Sub is at-most-once:
   the missed message cannot be replayed.

3. Instance 3 after T=40s:
   L1 TTL expires → cache miss → loads from Redis L2 or DB → ₹799
   From T=40s onward: Instance 3 serves correct price.

4. Maximum staleness window: up to 40 seconds (remaining TTL)
   Worst case: miss happens with full TTL remaining (60s window)
   This is the designed trade-off: Pub/Sub + TTL-based fallback

5. Reduce staleness window:
   a) Shorter Caffeine TTL: 10s instead of 60s → max 10s staleness
   b) Reconnect-triggered refresh: on Redis reconnect, evict all L1 entries
      (conservative: clear L1 on reconnect → fresh loads after disruption)
   c) Shorter reconnect timeout: detect disconnection faster

test_cases.expected:
1. Instances 1, 2, 4, 5 → immediately serve ₹799 (L1 evicted + fresh load)
   Instance 3 → still serves ₹999 (stale L1, missed invalidation)

2. Instance 3 between T=0 and T=40s:
   Serves STALE ₹999 from L1 Caffeine cache.
   The cache TTL countdown continues — it was at 40s when the miss happened.
   Instance 3 reconnected at T=5s but Pub/Sub is at-most-once:
   the missed message cannot be replayed.

3. Instance 3 after T=40s:
   L1 TTL expires → cache miss → loads from Redis L2 or DB → ₹799
   From T=40s onward: Instance 3 serves correct price.

4. Maximum staleness window: up to 40 seconds (remaining TTL)
   Worst case: miss happens with full TTL remaining (60s window)
   This is the designed trade-off: Pub/Sub + TTL-based fallback

5. Reduce staleness window:
   a) Shorter Caffeine TTL: 10s instead of 60s → max 10s staleness
   b) Reconnect-triggered refresh: on Redis reconnect, evict all L1 entries
      (conservative: clear L1 on reconnect → fresh loads after disruption)
   c) Shorter reconnect timeout: detect disconnection faster

hints: At-most-once: reconnecting doesn't replay missed messages
TTL is the fallback: staleness bounded by remaining TTL at time of miss
Reconnect-evict: clearing L1 on reconnect is a common defensive pattern

exercises.title: Razorpay merchant config invalidation via Pub/Sub
exercises.scenario: Merchant configuration changes must propagate to all instances immediately.
exercises.instructions:
Build Razorpay merchant config invalidation:
1. MerchantConfig record: merchantId, webhookUrl, maxPaymentAmount, enabled
2. MerchantConfigService: findByMerchantId @Cacheable, updateConfig (save + invalidate)
3. CacheInvalidationService: invalidate local + publish
4. Handler: evict on receive
5. @SpringBootTest test: update config, verify stale value evicted
hints: Local evict in updateConfig: synchronous, happens before publish
Thread.sleep(50): small buffer for async pub/sub delivery in test
CacheManager.getCache().clear() in @BeforeEach: clean test state

exercises.title: Session invalidation via Pub/Sub — logout broadcast
exercises.scenario: When a user logs out, invalidate their session on all instances.
exercises.instructions:
Write SessionInvalidationService:
1. Session cached in L1 Caffeine per instance: 'session:{userId}'
2. logout(userId): invalidate local session + broadcast
3. Channel: 'session.invalidate'
4. Message: userId
5. Handler: evict session from L1
6. Test: logout → session null on this instance + check broadcast was sent
hints: Logout is security-critical: must propagate to all instances immediately
TTL fallback too slow for security: use Pub/Sub for immediate propagation
Cache.ValueWrapper: Spring's nullable wrapper for cache.get(key)

exercises.title: Multi-entity Pub/Sub with routing handler
exercises.scenario: One container, multiple cache types, routing by channel.
exercises.instructions:
Build a universal CacheInvalidationRouter:
1. Handles channels: 'cache.products.*', 'cache.categories.*', 'cache.merchants.*'
2. PatternTopic: 'cache.*'
3. Routes to correct cache based on second channel segment
4. Supports specific key eviction AND full cache clear
5. Test: three different invalidations all route correctly
hints: implements MessageListener: raw interface, gets Message object with channel bytes
channel.split('\\.'): extracts cache name from channel position
PatternTopic('cache.*'): matches cache.products.*, cache.categories.* etc.

exercises.title: Reconnect-triggered L1 cache clear
exercises.scenario: When Redis connection is restored, clear L1 to avoid serving stale data.
exercises.instructions:
Write RedisReconnectListener:
1. Implement ConnectionEventListener or @EventListener for Redis connect events
2. On reconnect: clear all L1 caches (conservative, safe)
3. Log: which caches were cleared and when
4. Explain: why this is safer than trusting TTL after a reconnect gap
5. Test: simulate reconnect event, verify all caches cleared
hints: Conservative clear: safer than trying to replay missed events
Performance impact: brief L1 miss spike vs risk of stale data
getCacheNames(): iterate all configured caches to clear all

exercises.title: Complete Flipkart cache cluster — Module 18 synthesis
exercises.scenario: Full Redis stack: @Cacheable + Pub/Sub invalidation + rate limiting + distributed lock.
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Flipkart Redis layer:
1. ProductCacheService: @Cacheable findById + Pub/Sub invalidation on update
2. RateLimiter: INCR/EXPIRE, 100 products/min per userId
3. DistributedLock: catalog update runs once across cluster
4. PubSub: product invalidation to all instances
5. CatalogSyncJob: distributed lock + rate check + cache invalidation broadcast
6. Test (3 tests): rate limit enforced, catalog sync runs once, cache evicted on update

All 3 tests pass.
exercises.starter_code: // Complete Flipkart Redis layer — M18 synthesis
hints: Rate limit key must include timestamp/window or counter never resets
Distributed lock key includes jobId: unique per sync run
Thread.sleep(50): small wait for pub/sub async eviction in test
